title: The Private Security Events Database
abstract: Since the 1990s, the private provision of military and security services has become a common feature of local, national, and transnational politics. The prevalence of private security has generated important questions about its consequences, but data to answer these questions are sparse. In this article, we introduce the Private Security Events Database (PSED) that traces the involvement of private military and security companies (PMSCs) in events in Africa, Latin America, and Southeast Asia from 1990 to 2012. We describe the PSED project, highlight its descriptive findings, conduct a replication and reanalysis of Akcinaroglu and Radziszewski's contract data in Africa, and compare the two databases' coverage of Sierra Leone from 1991 to 1997.
methodology_summary: Standard news-based event data collection. Lexis Nexis and World News Connection English-language news searches with the terms 'private security', 'private military', and 'mercenary'/'mercenaries' for each country and the time period. Articles read by at least two human coders. An event is recorded only when (1) a PMSC is mentioned and (2) the PMSC is involved in one of seven event categories (routine work, demonstration, riot, strike, violence, crime, plot). Event-type categorization coordinated with the Social Conflict Analysis Database (SCAD) to enable cross-referencing. Per-event variables include location, dates, deaths/injuries, services performed, client type, abuse allegations, perpetrator and victim coding, DoD-style incident type, PMSC name and nationality, and a free-text description.
population_description: 1,288 reported PMSC events across three regions (Africa 57%, Latin America 29%, Southeast Asia 14%) over 1990–2012. Africa includes 53 sub-Saharan and North African states; Latin America includes 22 states from Mexico to Argentina; Southeast Asia includes 11 states.
key_limitations: Media-source bias: undercounts overall PMSC activity (only reported events appear) and overcounts violent events relative to mundane work; international news favors big or English-coverage events. Geographic scope excludes MENA, post-Soviet space, and OECD. Temporal coverage 1990–2012 only. Reporting allegations is not the same as substantiating them. Some PMSCs may operate covertly with no media trace. The PSED data manager URL (psed.siecenter.du.edu) was active at publication; sustained access is via Harvard Dataverse.
replication_status: Authors replicate Akcinaroglu & Radziszewski (2013) using PSED event counts in place of A&R's PMSC-count competition variable, on African civil wars 1990–2008 with Cox proportional-hazards models. Replication confirms A&R's correlation between PMSC presence and shorter wars but rejects A&R's competition mechanism: when PSED government-event counts and A&R competition are both included, competition loses significance.
data_availability: PSED data publicly available on Harvard Dataverse at doi:10.7910/DVN/ZKKOK7 (file: PSED 1990-2012 Feb 2019.csv). Data manager visualization at https://psed.siecenter.du.edu (institutional).

title: Private Military Companies, Opportunities, and Termination of Civil Wars in Africa
abstract: This article examines how private military companies (PMSCs) affect the termination of civil wars in Africa. The authors argue that PMSCs create opportunities for civil war termination by shifting the balance of power toward governments and by creating competitive pressures that incentivize efficiency and speedy goal accomplishment. Using an original dataset of PMSC activity in African civil wars from 1990 to 2008, the authors find that increased PMSC competition—measured as the number of distinct PMSCs hired by a conflict party—is associated with faster civil war termination.
data_availability: Replication data available as a supplementary zip via Sage Journals at https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/suppl/10.1177/0022002712449325/suppl_file/10_1177_0022002712449325.zip. NOTE: This URL is protected by Cloudflare bot challenge and cannot be fetched autonomously; a human must download the zip in a browser, unzip it, and supply the local path ar2013_cow_conflict_year.csv to the prepare_ar2013 playbook. The archive also contains ar2013_acd_conflict_year.csv (ACD-panel version used in the original AR2013 Models 3-4 but NOT in Avant & Neu's replication) and ar2013_codebook.txt.
methodology_summary: Cox proportional-hazards survival models on a conflict-episode-year panel. Unit of analysis is the conflict-episode-year (one row per conflict-year per belligerent dyad). The COW panel (ar2013_cow_conflict_year.csv) covers African civil conflicts 1990–2008 drawn from the Correlates of War Intra-State War dataset v4.0. Key independent variable is pmsc_competition (numgov: count of distinct PMSCs hired by the government side per conflict-episode). Outcome is conflict termination (terminate=1). Control variables include ethnic fractionalization (ethfrac), polity score, mountainous terrain (mtnest), conflict intensity, military personnel (milper), rebel size (rebelsize), proportion of forces (prop1), rebel support (rsup), government support (govsup), and GDP per capita (gdp). All continuous controls entered in natural log form. Models estimated with time-varying covariates; standard errors clustered on conflict episode.
population_description: African civil conflicts 1990–2008 coded from the COW Intra-State War dataset v4.0. One observation per conflict-episode-year per belligerent dyad (government versus named rebel group). The alternate ACD panel covers a broader set of conflicts using UCDP/PRIO Armed Conflict Dataset coding.
key_limitations: PMSC data collected by the authors using secondary sources and may undercount covert or sub-contracted PMSC activity. Competition operationalized as a count of distinct PMSCs rather than a market-structure measure, which may conflate scale with competition. Sample restricted to Africa. Avant & Neu (2019) subsequently show the competition coefficient attenuates to non-significance when PSED government-event counts are included as alternative operationalization.
replication_status: Replicated by Avant & Neu (2019) in Journal of Conflict Resolution 63(8): 1986-2006. Avant & Neu demonstrate that the AR2013 competition result is sensitive to operationalization: replacing pmsc_competition with PSED government-event counts (pmsc_government_event_count) produces larger and more significant hazard ratios, and in a horse-race model competition loses significance while government-event counts remain significant.
